subject: Physics
q:
Match the List I with List II

  List I   List II
A Intrinsic Semiconductor I Fermi-level near valence band
B n-type semiconductor II Fermi-level at middle of valence and conduction band
C p-type semiconductor III Fermi-level near conduction band
D Metals IV Fermi-level inside conduction band

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the List I with List II

  List I   List II
A Intrinsic Semiconductor I Fermi-level near valence band
B n-type semiconductor II Fermi-level at middle of valence and conduction band
C p-type semiconductor III Fermi-level near conduction band
D Metals IV Fermi-level inside conduction band

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) The Fermi-level in an intrinsic semiconductor is nearly midway between the conductive and valence band.So, A→II.

(B) In the case of n-type semiconductor, the Fermi level is present just below the conduction band. So, B→III.

(C) Fermi level in a p-type semiconductor lies close to the top of the valence band. So, C→I

(D) In metals and semimetals the Fermi level lies inside at least one band i.e., inside conduction band. So, D→IV.

subject: Physics
q: A voltage regulating circuit consisting of Zener diode, having break-down voltage of 10 V and maximum power dissipation of 0.4 W, is operated at 15 V. The approximate value of protective resistance in this circuit is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: A voltage regulating circuit consisting of Zener diode, having break-down voltage of 10 V and maximum power dissipation of 0.4 W, is operated at 15 V. The approximate value of protective resistance in this circuit is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
The maximum current through the Zener diode is determined by its maximum power dissipation: $I_{Z,max} = \frac{P_{max}}{V_Z} = \frac{0.4}{10} = 0.04$ A.
The protective series resistance must limit the current such that the Zener operates safely. Using Ohm's law across the series resistance and Zener when the Zener carries maximum current:
$R_s = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_{Z,max}} = \frac{15 - 10}{0.04} = \frac{5}{0.04} = 125$ Ω

solution:
When pn junction is forward biased resistance is low. When pn junction reverse biased resistance is high. Because of this reason, we can amplify currents.
For this reason, Transistors can be used as:-

  • An amplifier
  • A switch
  • Logic gates

If transistor is in active state, small signal current gain (current amplification factor) will be
question: For a constant collector-emitter voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, the collector current of a transistor reached to the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>, whereas base current changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> value. If transistor is in active state, small signal current gain (current amplification factor) will be
solution:
Given here, VCE=8 V, IC=6 mA from 4 mA and IB=20 μA to 25 μA

Current gain is the ratio of change in output current divided by change in input current. For common CE (Common Emitter) configuration, it is the ratio of change in collector current divided by change in base current.

Current gain βav=IC-'ICIB-'IB=6-4 mA25-20 μA=2 mA5 μA

βav=25×103=20005=400

subject: Physics
q: The photodiode is used to detect the optical signals. These diodes are preferably operated in reverse biased mode because
question: The photodiode is used to detect the optical signals. These diodes are preferably operated in reverse biased mode because
options: fractional change in majority carriers produce higher forward bias current
fractional change in majority carriers produce higher reverse bias current
fractional change in minority carriers produce higher forward bias current
fractional change in minority carriers produce higher reverse bias current
solution:
In photo–diode, the diode is connected in reverse bias, so initially the current will be very small. If the light is incident on the diode, its photons produce more minority charge carriers, which are called photo-generated charge carriers, due to which the reverse current increases.

In reverse bias, the width of the depletion region is more and the current is already very small, So the fractional charge in the current due to the light will be large.

q:
Consider the following logic circuit.

question:
Consider the following logic circuit.

The output is $\mathrm{Y}=0$ when :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Y}_1=\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{Y}_2=\overline{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{B} \\ & \mathrm{Y}=\overline{\mathrm{Y}_1 \cdot \mathrm{Y}_2}=\overline{\mathrm{Y}}_1+\overline{\mathrm{Y}}_2 \\ & \mathrm{Y}=\overline{\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{~B}}+\overline{\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}} \\ & \mathrm{Y}=\overline{\mathrm{A}}+\overline{\mathrm{B}}+\mathrm{A} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{~B}}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline A & B & Y \\ \hline 0 & 0 & 1 \\ \hline 1 & 0 & 1 \\ \hline 0 & 1 & 1 \\ \hline 1 & 1 & 0 \\ \hline \end{array}$

q:
Statement I : To get a steady DC output from the pulsating voltage received from a full wave rectifier we can connect a capacitor across the output parallel to the load RL.
Statement II : To get a steady DC output from the pulsating voltage received from a full wave rectifier we can connect an inductor in series with RL.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Statement I : To get a steady DC output from the pulsating voltage received from a full wave rectifier we can connect a capacitor across the output parallel to the load RL.
Statement II : To get a steady DC output from the pulsating voltage received from a full wave rectifier we can connect an inductor in series with RL.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
solution: We can improve the average, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mi>C</mi></math> output of the rectifier while at the same time reducing the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> variation of the rectified output by using smoothing capacitors to filter the output waveform. Smoothing or reservoir capacitors connected in parallel with the load across the output of the full-wave bridge rectifier circuit increase the average <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mi>C</mi></math> output level even higher as the capacitor acts like a storage device as shown below. Both Statement I and Statement II are true.

subject: Physics
q: If the ratio of the concentration of electrons that of holes in a semiconductor is $\frac{7}{5}$ and the ratio of currents is $\frac{7}{4}$, then what is the ratio of their drift velocities?
question: If the ratio of the concentration of electrons that of holes in a semiconductor is $\frac{7}{5}$ and the ratio of currents is $\frac{7}{4}$, then what is the ratio of their drift velocities?
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{7}{5} \frac{\mathrm{l}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{l}_{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{7}{4}$
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\mathrm{e}}}{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\mathrm{n}}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{l}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{l}_{\mathrm{n}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{5}{4}$
